text: As we enter, you can actually see the collapsed left flow of the lung.
And the first step of the procedure is the division of the inferior pulmonary ligament.
To do this, we use the mechanical energy device.
By the gentle retraction of the left flow of the lung, the tension is applied to the inferior pulmonary ligament and is divided.
And the next step is the opening of the mediastinal pleura and identification of the anterior vagus.
As you see, the mediastinal pleura is opened up and is lifted up with the blunt instrument.
And we can actually start to visualize the whitish cord like structure, which is the anterior vagus.
With the help of the dythic instrument, the segment of the vagus nerve is cleared up from the peri-esophageal attachment, so it is just about two centimeters.
Once the vagus nerve is freed up from the peri-spagial attachment, it is lifted away from the vagus and clips are applied and it is cut.
This cut segment of the anterior vagus nerve is in the perform section.
The next step of the operation is identification of the posterior vagus.
Today, this peri-spagial tissue is held with an atraumatic grasper and its vagus is lifted up and is rolled towards the right of the patient.

With the help of a dissecting instrument, a peri-esophageal tissue is dissected along the posterior wall of the esophagus and we start to identify the white corollary structure which is the posterior vagus.
The posterior vagus is freed up from peri-esophageal attachments for a distance of 2 cm.
In a similar fashion as the anterior vagus, the clips are applied and the segment is cut and sent to the frozen section.
So the last important step of this surgery is identification of any missed branches.
Truth is, this vagus is circumficially dissected for about two centimeters.
As you see, the posterior dissection and anterior dissection, we couldn't identify any missed or anomalous vagus branches.
The last step of surgery is evacuation of air from left hemico-right.
To this, we introduce the red rubber catheter.

And as the lung is inflated, suction applied to the red rubber gets removed by continuous suction, thereby evacuating air from left hemisphere X. This patient had an uneventful post-operative recovery and was discharged post-op day one.
